ANDREW MACKENZIE, THE BARD OF DUNOVER,

Lattle is known of thq many minor poets of Ulster, and that little
i1s fast fading away. I take down from my shelves where rest a
hundred and fifty volumes of Ulster poetry, one—old and dilapidated.
My grandfather, David Bigger, subscribed for it in 1810, and it is here
still. Tt is entitled * Poemg and Songs on Different Subjects,” by
Andrew MacKenzie, Belfast, 1810. The frontispiece is a quaintly
engraved portrait of the poet, a la the ‘‘ Ayrshire Ploughman,” with
this verse beneath it:—

‘“ As some rude bird amid the trackless wild,
Its inharmonious notes pours on the breeze;
So I have oft the tedious hours beguild’d,
With scarce a hope or ¢’en a wish to please.”’

The dedication is ‘‘ to an indulgent and generous public,”’ and the
preface is humility 1tself. ‘‘ Even some of the most respectable ladies
and gentlemen of the North of Ireland have condescended to solicit
others in their own sphere to become subscribers,” of which there is
a list of about seventeen hundred!

MacKenzie was born at Dunov er, Co. Down, in 1780, the eldest
child of his parents, and at an early age was apprenticed to the weav-
ing trade, at which he afterwardi earned his livelihood. They were
stirring times in which the youth grew up. The Ards men were
" united,” almost to & man. We know not whether out poet was
“‘up,” as the phirase went , but it is evident from his poems and notes,
that he was well acquainted and in sympathy with those who w<re,
and in one case, to be noted, sang their dirge. His own Presbyterian
Winister, Rev. Robert Gowdie, was executed at Newtownards in 1798,
for his part 1n the insurrection. He was a friend of James Russell,
Who owned the inn at Groomsport, where his ’98 flint gun was long
Preserved, He also knew his servant, Ouidé Butler, who carted pro-

guns; and ran still further with his horse and cart as well, never tq
return when his master upbraided him for his cowardice! He also
knew old Bob Campbell who lived a quarter of a mile on the Bangor side
of Groomsport, hé who was in charge of the people’s cannon on g clog
wheel car at Ballynahinch, and from a height kept harrying the

' and when disaster came, was spared by an officer for

‘‘ reed-coaits,’
being ‘‘such a valiant cld fellow.”” He lived and died as ‘‘ the old boy
of the gap.”” He also knew ‘' Young McCance,”” who held the Wind-
mill Hill so heroically for General Munro at the battle of Ballynahinch,
for he is buried with his people in the quaint old churchyard of Temple-
patrick, with its ruined resurrection vault, on the shorcs of the sea near
Millisle.

In the old grave-yvard at Whitechurch rest many of the insurgents
who fought at Ballynahinch'ard Newtownards in ’98. Two of them
are recorded in stone as follows:—

‘“ This stone was erected in memory of Hugh and David Maxwell, of
Bally-walter, whose bodies are here interr’d. They fell in an attack
raade on the town of Newtownards, the 10th of J une, 1798.”

‘“ Lo Erin’s gerius hov'ring o’er their tomb,
With mourrful eye surveys the hallow’d sod ;
Where sleep her bravest sons in Earth’s dark womb,
She weeps ; Hopa whispers, ¢ Cease, they dwell with God.” ”’

The verse is McKenzie’s, and 1s given in his poemis.

The early poetic effusions of our bard appeared over the pen-name
of * Gallius,”” in the *‘ Belfast News Letter,”’ and this—his first col-
lected volume—was highly spoken of by his contemporaries. In
it are two poems addresscd to Robert Anderson, another bard who
was a designer for a number of years in my grandfather’s mill at Carn-
morney, after he came from Cumberland. He was the ‘‘ Cumberland
Bard,”” with a monument to his fame in Carlisle Cathedral, and his
works recently (1904) gathered together and published at Ulverston.
I wrote a sketch of him in the * Ulster Journal of Archaology,’”’ vol. V.
p. 100. MacKenzie has an enthusriastic note concerning him 1n his

appendix., For some reason I cannot learn, MacKenzie was evicted

from his humble.home by his landlord, and found refuge in Belfast,
where he had two sons, printers. Some of his admirers bestirred them-
selves to obtain him employment. They succceded—in getting him

appointed head of the then sanitary department of the Corporation—

chief scavenger. In 1823 he published another volume, ‘‘The Magonic

Cha.plet and other Poems.”” The poet died on 10th May, 1839, and was
puried in Shankill graveyard, Belfast, where a monument was erected
' to his memory by a fellow pcet, William McComb. It had on it the
lines— ' .
““ MacKenzie, O MacKenzie, thy spirit’s passed away
And left the earthly tenement to moulder in the clay.”’
I must look for this monument and see to it. Immediately before his
~ death the poet had sunk into poverty. I have a leaflet published at this
time with the somcwhat canting note below a hymn evidently written
by someone who had been influencing the dying man. It runs:—‘‘The
author of the foregoing hymn is a poor labouring man now in his 60th
~ year; he possessed some literary abilities, but unforfunately courted
~ the applause of the thoughtless multitude; and too shamefully neg-
% ‘Jected his soul’s immortal welfare. A severe and lingering illness has
Ah - at length awakened him to a sense of his situation, and he wishes this -
{  brief statement of his present religious impressions made public. It
';f‘i'?, s hoped bhat.mahy' who are r-e-ally Christians will take a copy at the
| moderate price of one penny.” His friend and contemporary, David
- Herbison lamented his loss in an elegy one verse of which runs as
follows :—
‘““For he was blithest of the throng
That sung where Lagan rolls along,
Where oft his lays, so sweetly mild,
The weary heart of care beguiled.”’

Several printers of the name of ‘‘ Bull’’ were given on page 39 of
the first volume of the I.B.L. (e.g., Joseph Bull, of Parsonstown, 1810).
Mr. Dix also drew my attention lately to the imprint on a small leafiet
(N.D.) which had come into his hands, viz:—'* Dublin: Printed by G.
Bull, 3, Redmond’s Hill,”” and to the fact of the name ‘‘ George P. Bull,
Printer,”” appearing in Wilson’s Dublin Directory for 1829. Since then
I have made a careful sedrch as to the name im all the ssues of Wil-
son’s Directory obtainable in the National Library, Dublin, where the
series, though very defective as to the early editions,t is better than
that in either the R.I.A. or T.C.D. The result of my search was as
followw :—

1813 and 1814—*‘ Bull (John), Printer, 10, gt. Strand-street.”” (The

John Bull, of Waterford, seems to have been printing at Waterford in

1810, and I have volumes printed by him there in 1816 and 1819.)

1826, 1827 and 1828—‘‘Bull (George), Printer and Stationer, 53,
Mary-street.”” (Is this the George Bull of 3, Redmond’s-hill, noticed
above ?) ‘

1829, 1830 and 1831—** Bull (Geo. P.), Printer, Bookseller and Sta-

. tioner, 40, S. gt. Georgels-street.’”’ (Is this the Roundwood printer,

the latest known printing at that village having ‘1820’ as the date?)

1832—‘“ Bull (Edward), Printer, 5, Redmond’s-hill.”

1835—** Bull, Edward, Printer, 15, Richmond-st. 8.

Bull, of 15, Richmond-st., South, appears as a printer, and this directory
continues his name as *‘ printer ”’ up to 1837, but the entry does not
4Dpear in the next three years.

IRISH TEXTS SOCIETY.

The annual meeting of this Society was held at 20, Hanover-square,
London, Mr. James Buckley, M.R.I.A., presiding. The 14th annual
report, was read by Miss Hull as follows :— During this year the nev
odition of O’Rahilly’s Poems, edited by Rev. P. S. Dineen and Mr.
Tadhe O’Donoghue, has appeared, forming the Society’s Volume. for
1909. It contains a considerable body of new material both in the text
itself and in the accompanying elucidatory matter. The second volumé
of O’Bruadair’s Poems, under the editorship of Rev. John Maclirlean,
is now completed. The third and final volume will then be proceed-'ed

with immediately. The second volume will form the Society’s publica:

tion for 1910. The Council have received offers of several new under-
takings during the year. They have received from Mr. J. C. 0’ Keefe
the offer of an edition of an interefsﬁng middle-Irish romance called
¢« Buile Suibhne Geilt,” or the ‘* Frenzy of Suibhne,” being the advel
tures of a prince of Dalaradia, whom the horror and bloodshed of the
battle of Magh Rath drove out of his sensew. It contains goeveral fine
poems. This work, which is quite completed, is now in the press.

Tadhg O’Donoghue has offered the Society an edition o

poems, accompanied by a prose tract connected with the !
Clandeboy from the Reéves Collection 1n the Royal Irish Academy:

Professor John
M’Neill has offered to write an introduction to this work. Besides
father MacErlean’s and Mr. O’Keeffe’s volumes going through the
press, the following reports have been received :(—Mr. J. H. Lloyd re-
ports that he has compared his copies of the poems from the book of
the O’Byrne’s from the T.C.D. manuscript with the photographs of the
same poems from the excellent Harvard manuscript supplied by Pro-
fessor F. N. Robinson, of Harvard University. This manuscript con-
tains three additional poems to those in the T.C.D. copy, and completes
one poem 1ncompiete in. the Dublin copy. Miss Eleanor Knott reports
that she has copied and collated all the available copies of the poems of

1 Teigue ‘‘ Dall”’ O’Higgin, bui has not yet begun to translate them.

Mr. Thomas O’Nowlan reports that he has collated many of the poems
for his edition of the ¢ Contention of the Bards,” and hopes to pro-
ceed upon the work with vigour in the autumn. He is founding his
edition on a good text by Bernard Na Cuirnin, in the Maynooth Col-
lection, but the large number of manuscripts to be collated makes his
work unusually laborious. A report had also been received from Rev.
George Calder saying that he had now cleared the way to proceed con-
tinuously with his work on his proposed edition of the Irish version of
the Thebaid of Statius. The Council have been much encouraged by

dthe ‘recent offer from one of their members, Mr. P. O’Kinealy, of a

generous donation of £125 towards the production of the long-desired
final volume of Keating’s ‘‘ History of Ireland.’”” This volume, to com-
plete their edition, will contain indices of names and of places mentioned
In the body of the History, with so far os possilbe, identifications of
Place names and the modern equivalents of personal names; also a map
of early Ireland, and the genealogies with which Keating concludes
his work., The editor of Volumes II. and III., Rev. P. S. Dineen, M.A.,
has undertaken this work, and it is hoped that members who already
POssess the three Volumes of the ‘‘ History of Ireland ’ will have the
Satisfaction of receiving this further volume without loss of time.

Mathew Carey, born in 1760, a member of a well-known Dublin
family, was bound to a bookseller, which included printing, and was
soon to learn the risks a clever writer runs who attacks the powers that
be. When only eighteen his first pamphlet, an appeal to his fellow
countrymen urging unconditional repeal of the Penal Code, and “ «x-
citing them to a just sense of their civil and religious rights,’”’ raised
such a storm that his friends shipped him to France. Here he was en-
gaged by Benjamin Franklin to reprint his dispatches from America,
through whom he made the acquaintance of Lafayette, and finally
worked for the younger Didot, the greatest printer of his time. Re-
turning to Dublin, his father assisted him to start ‘‘The Volunteer’s
Journal,” in October, 1783, an article in which denouncing the Gov-
ernment and all its works, led to a month’s imprisonment in Newgate.
Dreading further trouble, his friends persuaded him to emigrate to
America in September, 1784. He landed at Philadelphia, unknown and
friendless, with only a dozen guineas in his pocket, but a timely loan
from Lafayette of 400 dollars enabled him to start the * Pennsylvania
. Herald,” in 1785, and thenciforward, until his death in 1839, he be-
came the leading publisher in America, founding a firm which still
exists, and has had business relations with all the great English authors
of the past century.

‘As might be expected, Carey took an active part in all public move-
ments in the land of his adoption, but it is as the writer, not the pub-
lisher or publicist, that Carey interests us on this side. As his bio-
grapher says, ‘‘The feeling of love for his mothen country and of loy-
alty towards his people was never ohscured. It was this feeling that
impelled him to undertake to defend Ireland and the Irish against the
unparalleled libels and calumnies which had so long filled the page®
of English Histories of Ireland. Filled with a burning sense of indig-
" nation at the wrong done his countrymen, he had long planned a work
entitled ‘ Vindiciee Hibernicee: or Ireland Vindicated,” and went into-
the matter of preparation with great thoroughness. He purchased all
the books that seemed to have g bearing on the subject, even buying
" a share in the Ncw York Library and borrowing everything available:
Then he spent six months going through the books and liberally mark:

At a reoent ‘“Tea Table Talk ”’ the Countess of Desart read an in-
;oere‘sting paper on ‘‘Capt. Otway Cuffe and his work at Kilkenny,”’ in
the course of which: she said:—The death of the late Earl of Desart
without a son, and the present earl heing also without male issue, made
Capt. Cuffe immediate heir to the earldom and estate, and brought home
to him the possibility of his some day having to' take the responsibility
of that position. The Desarts have never been absentees; they have
always spent most of their time in Ireland since Henry Cuffe migrated
there in the time of Elizabeth. The owners of Cuffe’s Desert, as it was
originally called, always made it their home, and Otway Cuffe felt that
bis proper place was in Ireland. He found a charming home on the banks

of Nore, and looked around for some good work to do. He started a

soclal and gymnastic club for the artizans’ sons in Kilkenny, providing
a house and gymnasium with all necessary accescories. He next devoted
his energies to bringing North and South together, his dream always

“being a United Ireland, working in harmony for the prosperity and hap-

piness of the Motherland. Through the Gaelic League he had been
brought into contact with workers in Belfast, and one fine day some
dozen lads from the North camped in tents at Sheestown House, and
made, friends with boys of their own age in the hitherto unknown South.
Mr. Standish O’Grady—that prose poet—wrote a masque round the
heroic figure of Red Hugh O’Donnell, and this the boys performed in
the open air before an admiring audience—a scene never to be forgotten.
Keen on the subject of agricultural revival, he next created a tillage
farm, and made the turning of the first sod an opportunity for resus-
Cltating the Samhain of Celtic tradition. The gymnasium he had built
Was capable of being converted into a theatre (considered by the profes-
8lon one of the best equipped in the South), a concert-hall, ball-room,
or meeting place, and as a theatre it has justified its existence from the
finst. Ttg boards are occupied by the best touring companies, and
mateur performances testify to the artistic temperament of the citizens,
Life now began to stir in the dry bones, and the citizens to recognise
POssibilities of work for the common goad, with no ulterior motive. An
old ruined mill on the banks of the Nore next msqplred Capt. Cuffe mth

bought quietly and a company formed with a capital of £15,000. The
Kilkenny Woollen Mills were opened on BEaster Monday by Mr. Bryce,
then Chief Secretary, and when I was invited to start the machinery I
felt proud not only of the occasion, but of the company, representing as

1t did all creeds and classes of the community. The mills started with

16 looms; to-day they have 42! I acquired a portion of land on the
opposite bank of the river, seven acres of which were allotted to him
for experimental cultivation of tobacco—it has turned out to be the
most suitable soil in Ireland for that purpose. The ripe leaf is cured on
the premises, and the buildings are used in winter for intensive cultiva-
tion, thus keeping the workmen employed all the year round. The
housing problem being acute in Kilkenny, Capt. Cuffe next proceeded
o erect cottages in stone, brick and concrete for the workers at Talbot’s
Inch, a pretty spot which would need the inspiration of a poet to do

justice to its natural beauties. Here 1s erected the big woodworkers’

factory originally started by the pupils of the Technical School, but
from want of business knowledge threatened with an early demise.
The boys appealed to the captain, at the time Mayor of Kilkenny, called
to the chair by acclamation—the first- non-Catholic to hold it for forty

_ years. He responded to their appeal, threw himself with his accustomed

zest into the business, obtained sufficient capital to build a worthy habi-

 tation installed with electric machinery and everything up-to-date. He

had experience of all sorts—including a strike in November, 1910, from
which he emerged somewhat saddened. The village of Talbot’s Inch
now consists of 28 cottages, housing about 120 people, and a Recreation
Hall with club-room, library and billard tables.

As President of the Kilkenny branch of the Gaelic League and the
Industrial Association, he worked incessantly. An organiser of Ifels-
anna, of Piper’s bands, of all things that make for the physical, mental
and spiritual uplifting.of his compatriots, he wore himself out in his
endeavours to make harmony and peace, prosperity and happiness.

Broken in health by overwork, which only hig intimates were awaro of,

he laid down his life for Ireland, and though his ashes rest far away
under the Southern Cross, I believe that his spirit still broods lovingly
over the land for which he struggled so gallantly.

This is a wonderful year for centenary celebrations, and not the
least; interesting should be that of Vincent Wallace, the famous com-
